I suspect that this deal makes it even more likely that Microsoft will make=
 future Activision games console exclusives (i.e. games that are not yet re=
leased). If we assume that Microsoft=E2=80=99s primary goal is to grow Xbox=
 Game Pass, then before this deal the company could count on having an excl=
usive to Call of Duty on a subscription basis, along with all of the other =
Activision titles; that=E2=80=99s a pretty highly differentiated feature, a=
nd a reason to buy an Xbox. Now, though, any Activision title could potenti=
ally be available on any cloud service with a subscription business model; =
the real differentiation will be the ability to play on a console, which me=
ans those future games will probably only be available on one console =E2=
=80=94 an Xbox. Granted, this actually isn=E2=80=99t that radical a proposa=
l: Microsoft has made new-to-the-world Zenimax games like Starfield Xbox ex=
clusives, so it would make sense that Microsoft would pursue the same strat=
egy here.

That noted, it=E2=80=99s not out of the question that Microsoft simply take=
s their win and abandons their ambitions to be a cloud platform or even a c=
onsole maker: could the company simply become a game publisher for Sony and=
 Ubisoft=E2=80=99s cloud service and whoever else wants to give it a shot? =
For the record, I do think this is unlikely, at least in the near-term (Mic=
rosoft will give Xbox Game Pass a shot), but it wouldn=E2=80=99t be irratio=
nal and may happen down the road, particularly once you realize the biggest=
 motivation for Microsoft may very well have been the price it paid.
